summary:
The Convening of the Estates-General (May 5, 1789 AD) was the immediate trigger of the [[event:107|French Revolution]] — France's first meeting of its representative assembly in 175 years, called by Louis XVI as the only mechanism capable of authorizing new taxes to address the monarchy's near-bankruptcy. The Estates-General traditionally comprised three orders: the First Estate (clergy), Second Estate (nobility), and Third Estate (commoners, representing roughly 97% of the population). The Third Estate delegates, many of them educated lawyers and professionals influenced by Enlightenment ideas, arrived expecting genuine consultation on France's fiscal crisis and constitutional future — and almost immediately collided with procedural questions (would the three estates vote separately, allowing the privileged orders to dominate, or together by head, giving the Third Estate a majority?) that crystallized into fundamental questions about sovereignty and representation.

When the king and privileged orders refused to accept the Third Estate's demands, the Third Estate delegates transformed themselves into the National Assembly on June 17, 1789, declaring themselves the legitimate representative body of the French nation — a revolutionary act of breathtaking audacity. When locked out of their meeting hall, they adjourned to a nearby tennis court and took the famous Tennis Court Oath (June 20), vowing to remain assembled until they had given France a constitution. Within weeks, the [[event:2353|storming of the Bastille]] (July 14) transformed constitutional conflict into open revolution. The Estates-General's convening thus serves as the conventional starting point of the Revolution — the moment when accumulated social tensions, fiscal crisis, and Enlightenment ideology converged into political transformation.
